entry.title: What to use as a Template (Pre Populated with Items) to be used for creating Sales Order in SAP B1?
entry.content:
Users have to create large orders from Quotations which contains consolidated quantities. 

i.e. Item A = 1000, Item B = 1000, Item C = 1000.

Sales Order require Groups or segments in it.

Type 1 

Item A = 100, Item B = 100, Item C= 100

Type 2

Item A = 200, Item B = 100, Item C =500

There could be 20 Types. 
thinking the efficient way possible. 

Can we Draft for this approach i.e. Create a Draft of Each type or a draft with all Types..
OR
Should we create another Quotation in required format.

Goal is to create once and use later several times.

Thankyou.

entry.title: SAP CAP: CDS Action with nullable array parameter
entry.content:
Dear Community,

I am implementing a Node.js CAP CDS Service based on an interface definition provided and specified by SAP. The interface definition requires an entity action with following parameter:

  • code: String
  • comment: String
  • reasonCode: String
  • parameters: array of Object with following attributes:
    • code: String
    • value: String

According to that we have specified the action in the cds file:

The metadata is generated as

So far everything is working as expected, but the interface defintion specifies the "parameters" as nullable and provides an example executing the action as:

When performing an action without a "parameters" value the odata v4 logic is raising an error:

Deserialization Error: Parameter 'parameters' is not nullable.

Raised in:

I have not found a way to mark the parameter as nullable. Did I miss something?

Thanks

entry.title: SAP Best Practices for SAP Integrated Business Planning: Sample planning areas
entry.content:
The SAP Best Practices for SAP Integrated Business Planning for Supply Chain (SAP IBP) comes with many assets to help you fasten and safeguard your implementation project. The better you understand the different assets, the easier you can take advantage of all the benefits the SAP Best Practices for SAP IBP can provide. In this blog, we'll explain the sample planning area and the related assets. 

You can generally set up a runnable system including preconfigured planning UIs using Excel Add-in or the FIORI-app Planner Workspaces, sample data, analytics, forecast models, and alerts with the SAP Best Practices assets. The assets are updated and tested every release to ensure compatibility with the latest release and high quality. 

Let's start with an overview of the different asset types. In general, we differentiate between assets available in the system directly (marked with the green box in the following chart) and assets you can download via the SAP Signavio Process Navigator (marked with the orange box). Today we're focusing on the green part of the explanatory chart: assets that are delivered as part of the solution. To understand the content of the orange box, please check out the next blog. 

 

As you might know, SAP IBP delivers several sample planning areas for specific use cases. They come with:

  • Attributes
  • Master data types
  • Key figures
  • Planning levels
  • Planning operators
  • Versions

For example, the sample planning area SAPIBP1 is a unified planning area that is delivered with SAP IBP and supports end-to-end business processes. It includes the following SAP IBP applications:

  • IBP for sales and operations
  • IBP for supply (w/o response)
  • IBP for inventory
  • IBP for demand

This sample planning area is the base for the time-series based solution processes delivered with the SAP Best Practices content. We update the sample planning area every release to ensure that the latest and greatest functionalities in the applications are working smoothly and the needed master data types and key figures are available. We're constantly improving and simplifying the sample planning area with new features in the calculations, for example like IBP_ADD_TIME_DIMENSION function in 2408. You can also use only a part of the sample planning area when you for example just need the demand part, or the inventory part. Why should you use the sample planning area SAPIBP1 as a base for your own implementation? Of course, you can create your own planning area from scratch, but this needs quite some time, knowhow and effort. We recommend you to use the sample planning areas as a base for your own customer specific planning area and just change and add what you need to change. 

Besides, you can get more assets when copying a sample planning area. You just have to click the checkbox "Copy Planner Workspaces, Analytics, and Alerts".

 

 

Which assets you get depends on the sample planning area. Currently, we provide here assets for the SAPIBP1 and SAP7F sample planning area - but there's more to come. 

After the copy of the sample planning area SAPIBP1, your new planning area already has 

  • Forecast models
  • Forecast error profiles
  • Segmentation profiles
  • Analytics stories
  • Alert definitions
  • Charts and dashboards
  • Preconfigured planner workspaces

When SAP7F is your source, the list is a bit different: 

  • Alerts
  • Charts and dashboards
  • Preconfigured planner workspaces
  • Procedure Playbooks
  • Analytics Stories (starting with 2411)

As with all assets, you can also adapt, change, and enhance these objects to your needs. If you don't need them, you can also delete them easily. Our recommendation is to use this feature because the deletion of not-needed objects is faster compared to creating them from scratch. 

Was this blog useful for you? You can continue your SAP Best Practices for SAP Integrated Business Planning for Supply Chain learning here.

And here you can find some general links: 

  • Back to the overview blog
  • Process Navigator 
  • Process Navigator direct link to SAP Best Practices for SAP Integrated Business Planning (latest version)

entry.title: SAP Best Practices for SAP Integrated Business Planning: Artificial intelligence (AI)
entry.content:
In the last blogs, you learned a lot about the benefits, the different assets, the sample planning areas, the unified planning process, integration and so on of SAP Best Practices for SAP Integrated Business Planning for Supply Chain (SAP IBP). Let's conclude this blog series with SAP IBP Artifical Intelligence (AI) and the embedded and side-by-side intelligent use cases, SAP IBP AI offers. It doesn’t need an extra explanation, that machine learning and AI can make your life easier and help you to optimize your supply chain planning, to drive planning accuracy and automation. With that, you can free up the capacity of planners to take care of critical topics instead of daily routines, you can lower safety stocks, improve your master data quality, improve forecast quality, and much more.

Let me also state, that this is just the beginning. You will see more use cases in the next releases, for example helping you to find your way in the system, understand the results of planning runs better, get intelligent proposals for critical situations, and much more.

There is a quite long list of already existing AI use cases, let me focus on a few with the related Best Practices solution processes – just to make you curious.

Master Data Anomaly Detection

To have correct and consistent master data is for sure one of the biggest challenges in IT. An easy way to improve the master data quality is to run a master data consistency job after the master data has been imported to SAP IBP. Machine learning can learn semantic rules to identify problems in master data and recommend values for correction. In the solution processes IBP – order-based planning inbound integration with SAP S/4HANA (2S4) and IBP – order-based planning inbound integration for deployment planning (4VA) we run the ML Master data Consistency job on attributes PRODGROUP and PRODTYPE of the data type PRODUCT. This is just one example and you can enhance it to your needs.

Automated cluster and threshold proposal in segmentation

ABC/XYZ segmentation is a method of grouping planning objects based on key figure values. There are two types of segmentation: 

  • ABC segmentation is the prioritization of planning objects based on their relative importance. In the SAP Best Practices solution process, the product combinations are categorized based on their revenue. For example, the products contributing to 70% of the revenue are classified as A-products. 
  • XYZ segmentation is the classification of planning objects based on their demand volatility. In the SAP Best Practices, the product combinations are categorized based on their actual quantity. For example, products with a rather constant demand are classified as X-products. 

Why is segmentation important? Segmentation helps you to generate more accurate results for demand planning, inventory planning, and sales and operations planning.

SAP IBP offers different methods for segmentation, and one of them is K-means. If you choose this method, the system uses machine learning to create segments as homogenous as possible. This is especially useful if you are not sure what thresholds should be defined for the segments.

Here is the link to the related solution process IBP – ABC-XYZ segmentation (1S6).

Change point detection

Time-series analysis and change point detection allow you to identify patterns in individual time series. This enables you to learn about major changes in the sales history and allows you to select the most suitable forecast algorithms.  

Time-series analysis is used to detect patterns in a time series. The most important patterns are Trend, Seasonality, Continuous, Irregular, and combinations thereof. The patterns are saved to an attribute called Time Series Property. 

Change point detection allows you to identify significant changes in a time series. There can be level shifts (when the mean of the time series values alters significantly) or trend changes (when the direction or slope of a trend alters significantly). 

Here is the link to the related solution process IBP for demand – time-series analysis (34Y).

Forecasting

In the solution process IBP for demand – demand planning (11V), we provide different forecast models, for example for A- und B- products, for C-products, and products with intermittent demand, with automatic outlier correction, and so on. One of them uses the following advanced forecasting algorithms:

  • Gradient Boosting of decision trees
  • Extreme Gradient Boosting 
  • ARIMAX

 We won't explain here in this blog the details of the different algorithms, you can use the links to the help portal if you need further information.

Demand Sensing

Similar to the gradient boosting of decision trees algorithm, demand sensing with gradient boosting is an ensemble machine learning algorithm that considers the information gained from various external signals when the sensed demand is calculated. It’s used in the solution process IBP for demand – demand sensing (11X).

Let me point you at the end to the blog of my colleague Laura Tozzo - Unlock your supply chain potential with SAP Integrated Business Planning AI - to get more examples of how artificial intelligence capabilities in SAP IBP significantly drive planning accuracy and automation.

Was this blog useful for you? You can continue your SAP Best Practices for SAP Integrated Business Planning for Supply Chain learning here.

And here you can find some general links: 

  • Back to the overview blog
  • Process Navigator 
  • Process Navigator direct link to SAP Best Practices for SAP Integrated Business Planning (latest ver...

entry.title: SAP Best Practices for SAP Integrated Business Planning: Assets
entry.content:
The SAP Best Practices for SAP Integrated Business Planning for Supply Chain (SAP IBP) comes with a bunch of assets to help you fasten and safeguard your implementation project. It helps to understand the different assets and how to use them to take advantage of all the benefits and use cases of the SAP Best Practices for SAP IBP. In this blog, we'll explain the various assets and asset types in more detail.

You can generally set up a running system including Planning UIs, sample data, analytics, forecast models, and alerts with the SAP Best Practices assets. The assets are updated and tested every release to ensure compatibility with the latest release and high quality. 

Let's start with an overview of the different asset types. In general, you can differentiate between assets available in the system directly (marked with the green box in the following chart) and assets you download via the SAP Signavio Process Navigator (marked with the orange box).

We explained the green box, means the sample planning areas with the related objects in the last blog. Let's start now with the orange box. 

First of all, you need to know, how to access the assets for the latest version. You can use this link to access the latest version of the SAP Best Practices for SAP Integrated Business Planning. In the upper right corner, there's a button "Download". With this functionality, you can download all assets in a .zip file. To do it this way is easier and more convenient than downloading the assets separately. 

In the .zip file, under IBP => Library, you can find the following folders: 

  • General => overview slide decks 
  • TestScripts => the test scripts for all solution processes
  • Configuration => all configuration guides
  • Setup =>  the technical assets needed to set up the system
  • Others => solution process flow diagrams in .svg and .bpmn2 format 

Let's start with the document assets. 

 

Let me briefly explain the nomenclature of the file names. The release is always part of the file names: IBP2408 for example, or IBP2411. We update all files every release, therefore please use the latest ones.

In the folder "General" you'll find the following documents:

  • IBPxxxx_Best_Practices_Scope_Presentation_EN_XX (in ppt + pdf)
    This document is great if you want to understand the scope of the SAP Best Practices package better, get an overview of the unified planning process and learn when and how to use it. It contains a lot of interesting facts, explains relationships between the solution processes, contains slides you might also use for presenting a specific topic to your colleagues, and much more.  
  • IBPxxxx_Detailed_SAPIBP1_Presentation_EN_XX
    This document is the best source if you want to understand the modeling of the sample planning area SAPIBP1 and the changes per release. It gives an overview about the master data types, the key figures, the planning levels, planning operators and general planning area settings. 
  • IBPxxxx_Preconfigured_Analytics_Stories_EN_XX. Zip
    This document includes an extract of the preconfigured analytics stories as pdf. With that, you can look at the stories offline and use that as a starting point when designing your own stories. 

In the folder "TestScript", you find all test scripts for the solution processes available as part of the SAP Best Practices content. The file names of the test scripts start with a solution process ID. The ID is a three digit combination of characters and numbers, for example 11V_IBP2408_BPD_EN_XX.docs. It's a nice help when searching for a specific solution process, but you don't have to remember them all 🙂. The abbreviation BPD means Business Process Document. The test scripts describe how to run the business processes in the system. It's very easy to follow, because they describe the process in a detailed way, explaining each step the user has to do, which app to use, where to click, what to enter. They show at least a straight forward process, but in some examples they also include different options or variants. We deliver the test scripts as word-files. The idea behind is, that you can reuse them, adapt them for your own projects. This is a general rule we're following. Except the SAPIBP1 documentation and the analytics stories pdf, all files can be reused, adapted and changed. With that we want to increase the reuse of the documents and fasten your implementation project. 

In the folder "Configuration", you find all information needed to set up the system by your own. 
The first document that you have to check is the IBPxxxx_Quick_Guide_EN_XX.docx. It includes all the steps you need to do when starting with SAP IBP. How to install the excel add-in for using the planning views and how to set up the integration to several systems like SAP S/4HANA and SAP Build Work Zone. The technical configuration described in this document is the base for the business configurations described in the set-up guides. The set-up guides starts with a three letter abbreviation, followed by the release, for example TSP_IBP2408_Set-up_EN_XX.docx. TSP stands here for Time-series-based planning. Here is the complete list of used abbreviations: 

  • TSP => Time-series-based planning
  • OBP => Order-based planning
  • S4C => Setup Instructions for Implementing IBP for demand – demand forecast for SAP S/4HANA Clou
  • MRO => Maintenance, Repair, and Operations (MRO)
  • DDR => Demand Driven Replenishment

These set-up guides explain how to set up e.g. the planning area, how to load sample data, how to create job definitions. Everything you need to do to finally run the processes in the system as defined in the test scripts.  

If you want to explore also other topics in SAP IBP, the how-to guides might be useful. They describe extra configuration and content that is a bit outside of the overall unified planning process flow. But still it might be useful for you if you want to deal with these topics. Again, they start with a three letter abbreviation, followed by the release, for example DBP_IBP2408_how-to_EN_XX.docx.

  • DBP: Driver based planning
  • EOQ: Enhance sample planning area SAPIBP1 with Economic Order Quantity (EOQ) and Sustainable Economic Order Quantity (SEOQ) for inventory optimization
  • PRO: Process Management for the Unified Planning Process
  • SOP: How to Use SAP IBP with S&OP License Only (SOP)
  • XPA: Running commercial planning and consensus net revenue planning (SAP Analytics Cloud) with SAP IBP  

Now, we'll take a closer look at the technical assets.

 

Let's start with the first folder "Setup". Here you find per sample planning area one .zip file, means for the sample planning areas SAP7F, SAP8, and SAP9. For SAPIBP1 you find two files. One for the processes with SAP S/4HANA and another one for the demand use case with SAP S/4HANA Cloud, public edition. The structure for each file is similar - but not every file and not every sample planning area has all the subfolders. 

  • Time_Profile: the folder includes the time profile for the years from 2014 until 2035 (before 2411 it covers the years from 2014 until 2028)
  • Planning_Views: here you can find the preconfigured Excel planning views. They're created according to the processes described in the test scripts and can be changed to your needs. The Excel planning views aren't the only possible UI, but still many planners are used to this UI. Therefore, we offer the Excel planning view (for example for sample planning area SAPIBP1 14 planning views), but also preconfigured planner workspaces. 
  • Master_Data: .csv files for master data (sample data). In the beginning of your project, it's often easier to run the processes in SAP IBP without configuring first the integration to an ERP system. To do so, we deliver .csv files to import sample data via the Data Integration Job app. We've defined sample supply chain networks, products, plants, DCs, customers ... everything you need to run the processes. You want to give it a different flavour, for example because you want to use your own product descriptions to make it easier acceptable for your team? Just take the related .csv file and change the data. To understand the structure of the sample planning area, check the appendix in the scope presentation. Here is an example:

 

  • KF_Data: csv files for transactional data (sample data). To run the processes without a running integration to an ERP system, you need not only master data, but also transactional, historical data. The name of the .csv files already gives the information on which planning level the data is provided. For example, WKPRODLOC means that the data is in weekly buckets on the product / location level. Also here, you can adjust or enhance the files according to your needs. 
  • Analytics: The folder includes a list of the existing analytics, planner workspaces, alert definitions, charts, and dashboards. 
  • Realignment _File: this file is specific to the new product introduction solution process in the SAPIBP1 sample planning area. 

The last folder is called "Others". This folder contains the process flow diagrams for all solution processes in *.svg and *.bpmn2 format. These formats allow you to reuse the process flow diagrams in a tool of your choice and adapt them according to your needs. 

Here is an example of the demand review process. 

 

Was this blog useful for you? You can continue your SAP Best Practices for SAP Integrated Business Planning for Supply Chain learning here. 

 And here you can find some general links: 

  • Back to the overview blog
  • Process Navigator 
  • Process Navigator direct link to SAP Best Practices for SAP Integrated Business Planning (latest version)

entry.title: SAP Best Practices for SAP Integrated Business Planning: How to get it
entry.content:
If you have read the other blogs about SAP Best Practices for SAP Integrated Business Planning for Supply Chain (SAP IBP), you know already a lot about the benefits, the assets, and the preconfigured processes. You know where to find all the assets (SAP Signavio Process Navigator), how to download them and so on. In this blog, you find answers to further questions related to your own SAP IBP tenants. 

Is the SAP Best Practices content already available in my test tenant? 

When SAP IBP Operations provisions a brand-new Test tenant (including SAP IBP Starter Edition and partner (Cloud Test & Demo) tenants), all time-series based planning and demand-driven replenishment solution processes of SAP Best Practices for SAP IBP are pre-installed.  The default option is to provision the new test tenant as a copy of our SAP Best Practices for SAP IBP "master" tenant. Please be aware that the preconfigured analytics stories are not pre-installed. The order-based planning solution processes with planning area SAP7F need to be installed manually by the customer. 

In some cases, Test tenants may be provisioned without SAP Best Practices for SAP IBP.  Examples include: where a Test tenant is copied from another Test tenant (e.g. Test to Development); tenants that have been migrated from older versions of SAP IBP (e.g. SAP IBP release 4.0 or SAP IBP release 5.0); tenants that have been migrated from one data center to another, and in some cases of system landscape extension. For example, you received your system before 1908 and did not migrate to Normalization; if you order a system landscape extension, this will be delivered w/o SAP Best Practices content. 

Production tenants do not have SAP Best Practices for SAP IBP pre-installed, but are instead delivered as "blank" tenants.  This is because Production tenants are populated directly from Test tenants using the transport functionality.

What is the pre-installed planning area called?

Starting with SAP IBP release 1808, the pre-installed planning area with the SAP Best Practices for SAP IBP content will be named "UNIPA". The description of the planning area includes the release, for example Unified Planning Area for 1808.

For the SAP IBP releases 1708 - 1805, the pre-installed planning area with the SAP Best Practices for SAP IBP content was named "UNIPA" + the SAP IBP release number (e.g., "UNIPA1708", "UNIPA1711").

Planning areas for previous SAP IBP releases were named as follows:

  • IBP release 1705: UNIPA07
  • IBP release 1702: UNIPA06
  • IBP release 1611: UNIPA05

How can I verify whether the Best Practices content is installed, and whether it is up-to-date?

The best way is to use the Configuration app under Planning Area and Details and search for an active planning area starting with "UNIPA". 

If there is no "UNIPA" planning area, then the SAP Best Practices for SAP IBP have most likely not been pre-installed when the tenant was provisioned.

If you do find a "UNIPA" planning area, the SAP Best Practices for SAP IBP have most likely been pre-installed when the tenant was provisioned.  The description of the planning area will tell you the version (see above).  For example, planning area "UNIPA07" means that SAP Best Practices were pre-installed with SAP IBP release 1705 and the associated SAP Best Practices content.

How can I log on and view the SAP Best Practices for SAP IBP content?

The pre-installed content is created in the "master" tenant with the business user "UNIPA".  This business user should also exist in a Test tenant with the pre-installed content.  Therefore, you can copy the "UNIPA" business user's profile to your own business user, or even create an SAP Cloud Identity (SCI) user ID and assign it to the "UNIPA" business user.

Once I log in, where can I find the SAP Best Practices for SAP IBP content?

The pre-installed content can be found in the dedicated Fiori apps:

  • Charts in Advanced Analytics (starting with SAP IBP release 1705);
  • Dashboards in Advanced Dashboards;
  • Forecast models in Manage Forecast Models;
  • Segmentation rules in Manage ABC/XYZ Segmentation Rules;
  • Customer alerts in Define and Subscribe to Custom Alerts;
  • Planning view templates in Excel IBP ribbon under Template Admin → Templates; etc.
  • Planner Workspaces

Is there any special configuration for the analytics content?

There are two global parameters that control the visibility of charts and dashboards: CHARTS_PUBLIC and DASHBOARDS_PUBLIC.  Both parameters are set in the Configuration app under Global Configuration.  If set to "false", only those charts or dashboards that were created by or shared with your own user are visible.

There are several ways to view the pre-installed charts/dashboards:

  • Add your user to the user group UNIPA (preferred option); or
  • Set the global parameters mentioned above to "true"; or
  • Log on with the "UNIPA" business user (see above); or
  • Change the owner or share the charts/dashboards in the Content Administration app (this option is available starting with IBP release 1708).

Is the SAP Best Practices for SAP IBP content updated with each SAP IBP release?

The content available via the SAP Best Practices for SAP IBP package is updated with each SAP IBP release.

However, the SAP Best Practices for SAP IBP content that has been pre-installed or added in your IBP system is not automatically updated with each IBP release.  The SAP Best Practices content will remain in the state as when the tenant was first provisioned or when the content was added.  If you want the SAP Best Practices content to be updated to the latest version, you will need to manually install the latest content (see next question below).

How can I install SAP Best Practices for SAP IBP?

If the SAP Best Practices for SAP IBP have not been pre-installed when the tenant was provisioned, or if the SAP Best Practices content is from a previous release and needs to be updated, it is possible to manually install the latest SAP Best Practices content.

Download the SAP Best Practices package, with all documentation of how to install the content, from https://me.sap.com/processnavigator/SolS/EARL_SolS-034 (click on the asset "Download all Accelerators for SAP Best Practices for SAP Integrated Business Planning" under assets).

The package includes all installation instructions, including how to create the planning area (by copying sample planning area SAPIBP1), how to upload sample data and how to install all the additional application content.  This should take approximately 2-3 days' effort for all IBP components (less if you pick and choose only certain components).

What is the difference between sample planning area SAPIBP1 and UNIPA? 

​The sample planning area SAPIBP1 is: 

  • Delivered as part of the SAP IBP solution, like all other sample planning areas (SAP4/6/7/8)
  • Available in all SAP IBP systems
  • Used as a basis for creating custom planning areas
  • Not an active planning area that can be directly used to load data or execute planning processes; it must be copied to your own custom planning area
  • Covers the time-series planning processes for sales and operations, demand, inventory, and supply chain control tower
  • Updated with every release

​The planning area UNIPA is: 

  • Created as an active planning area by copying SAPIBP1
  • Pre-installed in newly deployed non-productive systems / starter edition / partner test, demonstration, and development systems
  • Includes additional configuration and sample data
  • Can be directly used for process testing, demo, and evaluation
  • Not automatically updated with each IBP release if pre-installed or added to your SAP IBP system

What is the starter edition and how to get it? 

The starter edition is a Proof of Concept tenant with an activated copy of the sample planning areas SAPIBP1 and SAP8 including the preconfiguration for the related solution processes and sample data. With the starter edition, you can immediately execute the sample end-to-end planning processes in SAP IBP without manual configuration. Here you find more information about the starter edition, like pricing, contract duration and so on.

What is the Cloud Test & Demo for SAP Integrated Business Planning and how to get it? 

The Cloud Test & Demo includes the same content as the starter edition, but it's only for SAP partners. Here the link to the details. 

And here you can find some general links: 

  • Back to the overview blog
  • Process Navigator 
  • Process Navigator direct link to SAP Best Practices for SAP Integrated Business Planning (latest version)

entry.title: SAP Best Practices for SAP Integrated Business Planning: Fundamentals
entry.content:
Welcome to the SAP Best Practices for SAP Integrated Business Planning for Supply Chain blog series! We're excited to share the fundamentals of this powerful package that revolutionizes how you implement SAP Integrated Business Planning for Supply Chain (SAP IBP). Whether you're a seasoned professional or new to the world of SAP Integrated Business Planning, this series is tailored to provide the information you need to benefit from  SAP Best Practices for SAP Integrated Business Planning.

Where are we? We (Ina Glaes and Berthold von Haaren) are working in Product Management for SAP IBP for several years and want to share our knowledge and experience about the SAP Best Practices for SAP IBP with you. 

The SAP Best Practices for SAP IBP are not new - you might know them already. In the meantime, we have published the 36th version of it. Every release, every quarter we deliver a new version that is based on the latest release of the solution SAP Integrated Business Planning. 

In this blog, we'll provide you with a comprehensive overview of SAP Best Practices for SAP IBP and its benefits. We understand that as someone implementing and using SAP IBP, you're looking for practical insights that can be easily applied to your daily work. So, grab a cup of coffee and get ready to dive into the world of SAP Best Practices for SAP Integrated Business Planning!

Now, let's outline the blog series to give you a preview of what's to come:

1. What are SAP Best Practices for SAP Integrated Business Planning? This first part is all about use cases and benefits. Here is the link.

2. Sample Planning Areas: What are sample planning areas, why are they useful, and what else can they provide to you? Here is the link.

3. Assets: The different assets delivered as part of the SAP Best Practices provides a lot of benefits. In this blog you will learn all you need to know about the assets and how to use them. Here is the link.

4. Unified sample planning process: What is the unified sample planning process and how could it help in your implementation project? Here is the link.

5. Integration made easy: This blog gives you more details on the integration options delivered with the SAP Best Practices content. Here is the link.

6. Artificial Intelligence: In this blog, you can learn how machine learning and artificial intelligence as part pf the SAP Best Practices help to optimize your supply chain. Here is the link. 

7. How to get it:  In this blog, you find answers to further questions related to your own SAP IBP tenants. Here is the link.

 

And here you can find some general links: 

  • Process Navigator 
  • Process Navigator direct link to SAP Best Practices for SAP Integrated Business Planning (latest version)

 

Let us know if you miss a topic!

entry.title: SAP Best Practices for SAP Integrated Business Planning: Integration made easy
entry.content:
In our series about SAP Best Practices for SAP Integrated Business Planning (IBP) for Supply Chain, we give you an overview specific to the SAP Best Practices for IBP. The series includes blogs about the assets and usage of SAP Best Practices. This blog gives you more details on the integration options delivered with the SAP Best Practices content.

As illustrated in the graphic above SAP IBP offers many interfaces to integrate data to SAP IBP. Some of the interfaces are managed by SAP only, but some interfaces are meant for customer usage. SAP Best Practices gives setup and configuration examples for the most common customer-managed interfaces.

This graphic is a simplified overview of the interfaces touched by the SAP Best Practices content. Several integration scenarios are delivered for several use cases. For sure the most important and prominent one is the integration to an ERP system like SAP S/4HANA. But as part of the SAP Best Practices content, you also find integration possibilities for example to SAP Analytics Cloud, enterprise edition or SAP Build Work Zone, advanced edition. In the following, we give you an overview of the most important integration scenarios delivered within the SAP Best Practices for IBP. Many more details can be found in the respective configuration guides available in the SAP Signavio Process Navigator.

 

  • CSV Upload
    For use cases such as study, training and demonstration sample data is delivered as part of the content for SAP Best Practices. A significant advantage of the CSV upload is that you can change the CSV files to your need. For example, you can easily change the description of the material. Also, no integration is necessary to an ERP system like SAP S/4HANA. (Note: CSV files aren't available for content based on sample planning area SAP7F). Master data such as location or products and transactional data such as historical data are delivered as CSV files. To upload the data, you can use the SAP IBP built-in Data Integration Jobs app. The files are located as technical assets in the the SAP Signavio Process Navigator.
  • Integration to SAP S/4HANA or SAP ECC using SAP Cloud Integration for data services (CI-DS)
    For the sample planning area SAPIBP1, SAP Best Practices content is available for the inbound integration of master data and transactional data. The integration uses templates delivered with SAP CI-DS and is based on the SAP S/4HANA, supply chain integration add-on for SAP IBP. The SAP Best Practices contents consist of integration examples for master data like locations or products and integration examples of transactional data like stock on hand or resource capacity. More information on the SAP CI-DS templates can be found in the application help.
    Also, an outbound integration is available to integrate planned independent requirements from the demand process back into SAP S/4HANA. 
  • Integration to SAP S/4HANA Cloud, public edition using SAP Integration Suite
    This integration connects the SAP S/4HANA Cloud, public edition to SAP IBP using SAP Integration Suite as middleware. As part of the SAP Integration Suite content so called iFlows are delivered. These iFlows handle the necessary inbound and outbound data integration between the two Cloud systems. This integration supports the SAP IBP demand process, which allows SAP S/4HANA Cloud, public edition to use the advanced forecasting algorithms available in SAP IBP. Detailed information about this integration is available here.
  • Integration to SAP S/4HANA using real-time integration (RTI)
    For the sample planning area SAP7F mainly RTI is used for the integration. This integration technology allows the transfer of master and transactional data in near real-time. This technology captures changes in real time and sends it asynchronously to SAP IBP.
    As a prerequisite you've set up several SAP Best Practices processes in SAP S/4HANA like the Sell from Stock scenario. Within the SAP Best Practices content, we're describing how to set up the real time integration profile and the necessary settings like Integration Model to exchange data between SAP S/4HANA and SAP IBP. For setting up RTI, we highly recommend the usage of Cloud Integration Automation Service (CIAS) developed by SAP. CIAS is a guided workflow that shows all the necessary steps to set up the integration. More information about this service is available here.
  • SAP Build Work Zone, advanced edition (Work Zone)
    SAP Build Work Zone is used as an optional tool for collaboration in the SAP Best Practices sales and operations (S&OP) planning process. Using the collaboration functionality offered by SAP Work Zone you can easily communicate about the progress of a business process or exception management. As part of the SAP Best Practices configuration, it's described how to set up the necessary communication system and arrangement to SAP Work Zone. More information is available in the application help.
  • SAP Analytics Cloud, enterprise edition (SAC)
    Besides the out of the box embedded SAP SAC content, we also ship SAP Best Practices content for scenarios using SAP IBP data in processes on SAP SAC. This integration allows you to run for example scenarios like using forecast from SAP IBP in sales and marketing planning or for alignment between financial, supply chain and commercial functions. We also deliver SAP Best Practices content for inbound scenarios to send SAP SAC data to SAP IBP.

The previous graphic is an example of the Integrated Financial Planning scenario. As illustrated OData services are used for the integration between SAP IBP and SAP SAC (and SAP S/4HANA). In the SAP Best Practices content, we're describing how to set up the communication between the two systems. Detailed information on scenario Integrated Financial Planning is available here. For scenarios Commercial Planning and Consensus Net Revenue Planning, which also communicates via OData services, additional information is available under the Tips & Tricks section on SAP Signavio Process Navigator.

This concludes our overview about the integration possibilities that we're delivering within SAP Best Practices for SAP IBP.

Was this blog useful for you? You can continue your SAP Best Practices for SAP Integrated Business Planning for Supply Chain learning here.

And here you can find some general links: 
Back to the overview blog
Process Navigator
Process Navigator direct link to SAP Best Practices for SAP Integrated Business Planning (latest version)

entry.title: What are SAP Best Practices for SAP Integrated Business Planning?
entry.content:
You might have heard about or even used SAP Best Practices for SAP S/4HANA. But are you aware that SAP also delivers SAP Best Practices for other solutions such as SAP Integrated Business Planning (IBP) for Supply Chain? In that blog series we want to give you more insights specific to the SAP Best Practices for SAP IBP. The series of blogs gives an overview about the assets and usage of SAP Best Practices and business processes supported in SAP IBP by SAP Best Practices.

In the first blog post, we want to give you an introduction about SAP Best Practices and the benefits it gives to you. In general, SAP Best Practices for IBP are standardized business processes, for example, for:

  • Sales and Operations Planning (S&OP)
  • Response and Supply Planning
  • Demand Planning
  • Inventory Optimization

The content is delivered via various assets and aims to serve as a starting point for your SAP IBP journey. The key benefits of using Best Practices are:

  • Fit gap analysis:
    Before you start with your implementation, you can use the predefined business processes to understand potential gaps within the solution. Also, you can use the business processes to learn how typical scenarios are modeled within SAP IBP. 
  • Reduced Implementation Time:
    First, by using pre-configured content and guided configurations, you can significantly reduce the time required to implement IBP processes. Second, SAP Best Practices provides a sample planning area supporting different planning processes. Using the sample planning area as a starting point reduces the modeling effort. Also, you get content including planning views, analytic stories, alerts, and sample data.
  • Improved Quality and Consistency:
    Adopting proven methodologies and content like extensive documentation on the configuration and on the business processes for high-quality outcomes and consistency.
  • Risk Mitigation:
    By applying established SAP Best Practices processes, you reduce the risk of errors and implementation failures.
  • Faster Time to Value:
    On the one hand, you can quickly realize the benefits of your SAP IBP investment, with faster deployment of the business processes as a template for your company. On the other hand, it is important to know that you have the flexibility to extend the configuration and processes to meet your business goals.

 

How can SAP Best Practices now help on your concrete use case? The picture before gives an overview of the different use cases. Some of our customers are using SAP Best Practices as a starting point for study and educating themselves and using the materials for training purposes. Others use the SAP Best Practices content for a proof of concept or a pilot implementation. But also, many customers used it as a basis for their implementation project.

And the best I haven't t told you so far – all SAP Best Practices content is free of charge. You only need a user for the support applications in the SAP ONE Support Launchpad (aka S-User) to access the SAP Signavio Process Navigator, that’s all.

To summarize, SAP Best Practices give a strong foundation to implement SAP IBP business processes effectively. By applying pre-configured SAP IBP content, you can accelerate your implementation timelines, reduce costs, and achieve consistent, high-quality results. This approach ensures that you can quickly adopt SAP IBP and maintain a competitive edge in your supply chain planning journey.

Was this blog useful for you? You can continue your SAP Best Practices for SAP Integrated Business Planning for Supply Chain learning here.

And here you can find some general links: 
Back to the overview blog
Process Navigator
Process Navigator direct link to SAP Best Practices for SAP Integrated Business Planning (latest version)

entry.title: SAP Best Practices for SAP Integrated Business Planning: Unified Planning Process
entry.content:
In the last blogs, you learned about the different assets of SAP Best Practices for SAP Integrated Business Planning for Supply Chain (SAP IBP) delivered as part of the sample planning areas or downloadable from Process Navigator. This information lets you set up a system with various solution processes and sample data. But how do the processes fit together, how do they interact, how do they depend on each other? 

We combined the most important processes into a unified planning process flow, explaining the relationship of the processes to each other and the outside world (means outside of SAP IBP). 

 

 

So, what is the unified planning process all about?

It's an exemplary, integrated, end-to-end planning process that crosses all SAP IBP applications. It's based on comprehensive sample planning areas  - SAPIBP1 and SAP7F - supporting planning at different times and data granularity. The SAP Best Practices for SAP IBP package delivers corresponding preconfigured and validated content like planning views in Excel, analytics stories, alerts, and sample data. In an implementation project, often not all SAP IBP applications and processes are relevant. For example, you want to start with sales and operations planning (S&OP) and demand first. But even in this case, the related part of the unified planning process can be used. 

The process covers

  • Tactical planning, which is normally done monthly for a 1 to 3 years horizon
  • Weekly mid-term operational planning is usually done for the next 1 to 12 months
  • Short-term operational planning is done on a daily frequency for the upcoming 1 to 12 weeks

The process starts with the demand planning. The goal of this step is to gain full demand transparency for the upcoming demands. Based on historical sales data, the statistical forecast is the base for local demand planners to create the local demand plan, typically for a specific location product or product group. Based on the local demand plan and the last final sensed demand, the global demand planner creates a global demand plan.

In demand sensing, the sensed demand is calculated based on daily considering delivered quantities. The demand sensing forecast can be fed back into the collaborative demand planning process to allow demand planners to change the demand plans.

The next step, the demand review, is part of the Sales and operations planning process (S&OP). During demand review, representatives from sales, marketing, finance, and demand planning gather and review their various demand inputs. Examples of such data are the marketing plan, the sales plan, and the annual operating plan retrieved from external sources such as SAP S/4HANA. In the second step, they jointly define a single, combined consensus demand plan for measuring current volume and financial target achievement.

Inventory optimization is done weekly and optimize multi-stage inventory targets and inventory components to efficiently meet customer service levels. To do so, it calculates inventory targets like recommended safety stock for each material at each location in a supply chain. It also calculates the types of inventory that comprise the total inventory for a given item to support a more granular inventory optimization calculation. Examples here are target cycle stock or target onhand stock. It also considers and compensates for uncertainties like forecast error and supply uncertainty. The inventory plan serves as an input to the sales and operations planning process.

In the sales and operations process, the next step is the supply review. Here the supply planner creates a complete supply plan for the consensus demand. This plan defines for each product how much needs to be purchased, produced, or shipped at each location in the supply chain and in which period of the planning horizon. Using the time-series-based supply planning heuristic planning algorithm, the supply planner can generate an infinite supply plan with no shortages to identify supply and capacity bottlenecks.

As another option, the time-series-based supply planning optimizer planning algorithm generates a cost-optimized production, distribution, and procurement plan for the entire supply chain network, considering certain constraints. The result of the supply review, the constrained demand plan is then taken into the reconciliation review.

During the reconciliation review, representatives from sales, finance, marketing, production, and demand planning discuss deviations between constrained demand and consensus demand. They agree on one executive proposal and prepare a list of issues for escalation. Next, the executive management will decide on an executive proposal during the management business review.

The last step in Sales and Operations process is the management business review.

During management business review, the executive management of the involved business units determines if the presented proposal aligns with the company's financial targets and key performance indicators. They evaluate and decide on the presented list of escalated issues. The review results in an approved, final consensus demand and supply plan. This plan ultimately drive revenue growth and increase market share, optimize product and customer profitability, minimize inventory costs, and overcome capacity constraints.

Let's go back to the midterm operational layer and let's take a closer look at the supply and allocations planning. The supply and allocations planning creates a feasible supply plan and an allocation plan based on demand priority rules and supply chain constraints like resource capacity, supplier committed quantities, and safety stock targets. The planner use what-if analysis and root-cause analysis to understand why the demand is not fulfilled. The system shows, for example, the projected stocks for all location materials and their corresponding supply elements and gating factors.

Response planning allows the planner to react quickly to incoming customer orders. The planner creates order confirmations and an adopted supply plan based on prioritized demands, allocations, and supply chain constraints. Issues like stock shortages can be identified by reviewing the order-relevant planning information, like a detailed sequential view of the demand, or delays and gating factors. Simulation and collaboration capabilities are applied for further review and finalization of the supply plan. The creation of scenarios allows what-if analysis to simulate potential options. Based on a decision for a scenario, data can be updated in the back-end system to make the simulation model real. 

Deployment planning allows the planner to create a reliable short-term distribution plan considering also unforeseeable events in the supply chain. Alerts, for example for each location product when the value of the projected stock is negative, draw attention to a potential issue. The deployment run considers all customer demands (sales orders), all confirmed component demands, all forecasted customer demands, and safety stock. The run then tries to satisfy the demands with the existing supply elements. The stock transfer requisitions that are pegged to supply elements that are considered available to deploy become deployment stock transfer requisitions. These requisitions represent the short-term deployment plan. 

Transportation load building (TLB) helps to group planned distribution receipts for products with the same transportation lane, transportation group, and delivery data. There are two different use cases. In the first use case, TLB is a subsequent planning step after deployment planning. As a distribution planner, you first schedule the transportation load building job. This job generates transportation loads for deployable products that are to be transported between locations and consolidates deployment requisitions into loads. In the next step, the planner can review the loads in the Planner Workspace. The second use case consolidates purchased products into loads that are to be transported from suppliers to plants. The planner first creates a purchase requisition and then creates the load manually in the Planner Workspace.

You might recognize that not all solution processes available in SAP Best Practices for SAP IBP are captured in the unified planning process flow. For example, the integration solution processes, ABC/XYZ segmentation, forecast error calculation, time-series analysis, and so on are not represented here. These processes are important and needed to run the complete story, but it would make the picture too crowded and distract from the main flow. If you want to know the order of all solution processes, you can use the Solution Process flow slides in the Best Practices Scope presentation - you can find them in the appendix. Here's the slide for SAPIBP1-based solution processes - the numbers in the grey circle indicate the order in which the solution processes should be executed. 

Was this blog useful for you? You can continue your SAP Best Practices for SAP Integrated Business Planning for Supply Chain learning here.

And here you can find some general links: 

  • Back to the overview blog
  • Process Navigator 
  • Process Navigator direct link to SAP Best Practices for SAP Integrated Business Planning (latest version)

entry.title: Analysis authorizations created in T-CODE RSECADMIN do not work on Market Segment.
entry.content:
Dear All.

Requirement:
On the Market Segment app, we want to set permissions to only see certain CO areas.
Example - Set a role to be authorized to see only the data in CO area 1000.

Implementation Details:
(1) Set the characteristics of the CO area to the authorization-related characteristics.
(2) In T-CODE RSECADMIN, create an analysis authority that specifies CO area 1000.
(3) Assign the analysis authority in (2) to the role's authority object S_RS_AUTH.

Result:
On the Market Segment app, an error occurred that there is no authority.

Remarks:
When the authority object: S_RS_AUTH is set to *, the analysis authority "0BI_ALL" is also assigned, which means that data other than the CO area 1000 can be viewed.

Therefore, what should I do to make the analysis authority effective?
(I have also read about creating an authority variable (for the CO area) on the query side, but I don't know what to set for "Var Represents" when creating the authority variable).

Please let me know. Thank you in advance.

entry.title: Re: SAP Gateway Client error: "CSRF token validation failed"
entry.content: If you are testing in SAP Gateway client and getting the above error, please make sure to select HTTPS protocol..

entry.title: Issues with Data Blending in SAC Stories
entry.content:
I have two different Models in SAC and I want to combine this two model in a story and create charts and table visualization. 

Both Models has a shared dimension, In model 1 the dimension members are unique and in model 2 the dimension members are repeated. In SAC story, using link dimensions I have linked the shared dimension and I get a green tick there. Now comes the problem, when I try to create a chart  with linked dimension I am getting an error. It says  the linked dimension for this model is not included in the visualization dimension bindings.  Note: I am not using any live connection Models

Help me to figure out this issue and solve it. 

 

Thanks

entry.title: Re: fixed asset partial sell
entry.content:
Hi Erik

If my understanding of your business process is correct what you intend to do is feasible using fixed asset transactions

1-You do A/R Sales Invoice using the fixed Asset, Or alternatively using the Fixed Asset Transaction , Retirement with Sales, partial: you need to estimate how much of the asset is retired understanding that you retire Net Book Value, at the time when, according to the Convention for retirement set in the Depreciation Type assigned to the asset, the retirement should take effect, and proportional to the APC value set to be retired from the total APC of the asset.

2-After that you can create a new asset Master Data, and use the Asset Transfer, transaction for the rest of the asset.

 

Information about asset transactions in On Line Help

Home (OLH) >SAP Business One >SAP Business One 10.0 > Financials > Working with Fixed Assets > Asset Transactions >Transfer 

Jose

entry.title: Re: Task 9 - Using CQL in an unbound action (July Developer Challenge - "Reverse APIs")
entry.content:
Hello DJ,

Here is my implementation of the custom handler (notice the queries on lines 18 and 23) - 

this.on("selectProduct", async (req) => {
    const db = await cds.connect.to("db");
    const { Products } = db.entities("northwind");

    const { data } = req; //get the input data
    const communityid = data.communityid;
    // Convert input community Id into LowerCase and then derive its ASCII decimal value
    const commIdArr = Array.from(communityid.toLowerCase());

    let commIdAsciiVal = 0;
    for (x of commIdArr) {
      commIdAsciiVal += x.charCodeAt(0);
    }

    console.log("Comm ID Ascii Value: " + commIdAsciiVal);

    // determine total number of products in the db
    const totalProducts = await SELECT.one
      .from(Products)
      .columns("count(ProductID) as count");
    console.log("Total Products with alias: " + totalProducts.count);

    const totalProductsNoAlias = await SELECT.one
      .from(Products)
      .columns("count(ProductID)");
    console.log("Total Products No alias: " + totalProductsNoAlias.count);

    // Derive a 'logical' product Id from community id by taking Mod
    const commIdProductId = (commIdAsciiVal % totalProducts.count) + 1;

    console.log("Comm Id Prod id: " + commIdProductId);
    //Select the relevant product
    const result = await SELECT.one
      .from(Products)
      .columns("ProductName")
      .where({ ProductID: commIdProductId });

    console.log(result);

    return result.ProductName;
  });

and here is the CF logs output (notice the output on lines 3 and 4) -

:"POST /odata/v4/northbreeze/selectProduct ","type":"log"}
   2024-07-30T07:19:55.50+0000 [APP/PROC/WEB/0] OUT Comm ID Ascii Value: 1162
   2024-07-30T07:19:55.51+0000 [APP/PROC/WEB/0] OUT Total Products with alias: 77
   2024-07-30T07:19:55.52+0000 [APP/PROC/WEB/0] OUT Total Products No alias: undefined
   2024-07-30T07:19:55.52+0000 [APP/PROC/WEB/0] OUT Comm Id Prod id: 8
   2024-07-30T07:19:55.52+0000 [APP/PROC/WEB/0] OUT { ProductName: 'Northwoods Cranberry Sauce' }

Also, you can refer to my project on github at - https://github.com/slash-h/devchallenge-072024

Thank you.

entry.title: BTP integration with s4hana cloud using oAuth authorization Error - Failed to build headers
entry.content:
Hi,

I am trying to execute API of S4 Hana public cloud system from the BTP CAPM Application using OAuth authorization. I have used below SAP tutorial to achieve this requirement.
Tutorial link - https://developers.sap.com/tutorials/btp-app-ext-service-s4hc-use.html

Where as in this tutorial they are using Basic Authentication but in my scenario I am using OAuth authentication.

Created application is working fine when we test in from the BAS, but we are facing below error when we test the application from the cloud foundry environment.

Error - "statusCode":502,"reason":{"message":"Error during request to remote service: Failed to build headers. Caused by: AuthenticationType is \"BasicAuthentication\", but \"username\" and / or \"password\" are missing!"

Below is the screenshot of destination created. Note - Destination is created automatically using S4hana cloud extension 

 

Below is the JSON we have used to create instance of above service. Note we have followed steps give in sap tutorial https://developers.sap.com/tutorials/btp-app-ext-service-s4hc-use.html

JSON -{
"communicationArrangement": {
"communicationArrangementName": "SAP_COM_0697",
"communicationSystem": {
"communicationSystemHostname": "default.com",
"oAuthAuthEndpoint": "oauth.com/oauth/authorize",
"oAuthTokenEndpoint": "oauth.com/oauth/token",
"outboundCommunicationUser": {
"username": "****"
}
},
"inboundAuthentication": "OAuth2SAMLBearerAssertion",
"outboundAuthentication": "OAuth2mTLS",
"scenarioId": "SAP_COM_0697"
},
"systemName": "CPAPP_S4HANA_CLOUD_DEV"
}

Below is the screenshot of the Destination it has generated.

Below is the API which we are consuming from S4hana public cloud

API -    /sap/opu/odata4/sap/api_re_integrationobject/srvd_a2x/sap/reintegrationobject/0001

Below is the code written in CAPM application to call the read request of API, also this requesting  is routing through destination we created.

srv.on('READ', 'REIntegrationObject ', req => RealEstateIntegrationObject.run(req.query));

Note - This request works fine from the BAS, but getting error when we test if from cloud foundry deployed application.

Can you please help here to resolve this issue.

entry.title: Re: SAP CAP: CDS Action with nullable array parameter
entry.content:
We have changed the default nullability on collections from false to true in compiler release 5.0. Prior to that you can add an explicit `null` specifier like so:

service S {  action foo(p: many Integer null); };

which produces: 

<Action Name="foo" IsBound="false">
  <Parameter Name="p" Type="Collection(Edm.Int32)" Nullable="true"/>
</Action>

entry.title: Trouble Accessing Selected Value from Segmented Control in SAP MDK
entry.content:
Hello SAP Community,

I’m currently working on a project using the SAP Mobile Development Kit (MDK) and have encountered an issue with accessing the selected value from a Segmented Control. Despite following the documentation and various examples, I can’t seem to retrieve the value correctly.

 


// Function to update counters based on selected value
function Update_Frage_02(clientAPI) {
const selectedValue = clientAPI.evaluateTargetPath("#Page:Mobile_Evaluator_Fragen/#Control:FormCell_Segmented_02/#Value");

// Update counters based on the selected value
if (selectedValue === "Tage") {
clientAPI.getClientData().HybridAppCounter += 3;
clientAPI.getClientData().WebAppCounter += 3;
} else if (selectedValue === "Wochen") {
clientAPI.getClientData().HybridAppCounter += 3;
clientAPI.getClientData().WebAppCounter += 3;
} else if (selectedValue === "Monate") {
clientAPI.getClientData().HybridAppCounter += 3;
clientAPI.getClientData().WebAppCounter += 3;
clientAPI.getClientData().NativeAppCounter += 3;
}
}

entry.title: Re: Is the LGORT (storage location) data inside empty or...
entry.content:
Hi Yalcin,

My guess is, that your column LGORT allows null values and the value of LGORT in your example is null. You can check this in transaction SE11, if for this table the flag "Initial Values" is not set for LGORT. If you press F1 on this checkbox, you get an explanation also how you can fix it.

BR,
Alex

entry.title: Re: slow-moving indicator - wrong value
entry.content:
Dear @ondrej-532,

I don't think that there is an error in the calculation.

Assuming your 'Days with low consumption' is 15 and your reference day is today, the number of days (NODays) would be 16 instead of 15. The app selects the material with low consumption in the 15 days equal and prior to today's date. If you take those 16 days (as NODays) for your calculation, the result will be 5,14.

Hope that answers your question.

 

best regards

Marius

entry.title: Re: YCOA standard chart of accounts, environment: SAP S4 HANA CLOUD - Partner Demo Customizing?
entry.content:
Hi Dario,

The reason why Group CoA is active in your system is probably due to the fact that you have activated Scope Item 2U6 Group Reporting - Data from External Systems (2U6).
However, it seems as if at least some YCOA accounts are not assigned to an account in Group CoA yet. What I would recommend you to do for now is that you assign all YCOA accounts to a dummy Group Account assuming that you functionally don't need Group CoA as of now. You can do this via Mass Change functionality selecting all accounts assigning them to a dedicated account in YIKR.

 

 

 

 

 

Once that has been done, you can resume copying standard accounts as those now have an account in Group CoA assigned. Keep in mind that the account which you newly create from the existing account will have the same parameters as the original account including the account in Group CoA.

Kind regards
Johannes

entry.title: Re: SAP CAP: CDS Action with nullable array parameter
entry.content:
Hi @ckuczera ,

you can annotate the action parameter with the following:

@mandatory: false

This will add (or remove) the input validation for the annotated parameter, so you can omit it in the http request.

I don't think it will add the "Nullable" attribute to the metadata file tho...

BR Tom
